era_context: A pivotal year in Mediterranean and Near Eastern history. Philip II of Macedon is assassinated, and his young son Alexander III assumes power. The Achaemenid Persian Empire is in internal turmoil following the death of Artaxerxes II. In China, the Warring States period continues with Qin and Chu as major powers. Egypt remains under Persian control. This year marks the beginning of the end of the Persian Empire's dominance.

events.title: Assassination of Philip II of Macedon; Alexander III Assumes Power
events.description: Philip II, king of Macedon and architect of Macedonian hegemony over Greece, was assassinated at a festival in Pella by Pausanias, a member of the royal guard. The motives remain debated—personal grievance, political conspiracy, or Persian instigation have all been proposed. Philip's death created a succession crisis, but his son Alexander III (age 20) quickly consolidated power with the support of the army and key generals. Alexander secured his position by eliminating potential rivals, including his half-brother Arrhidaeus and his cousin Amyntas. This event fundamentally altered the trajectory of Mediterranean history, as Alexander would soon launch the invasion of Persia that would reshape the known world.

sources.name: Justin, Epitome of Pompeius Trogus, XI.2
Arrian, Anabasis of Alexander, Book I (brief mention)

events.certainty_note: The assassination of Philip II is attested in multiple independent ancient sources with consistent core facts. The date (336 BCE) is secure. However, the motives and any potential conspirators remain debated; ancient sources offer conflicting accounts, and modern scholars continue to discuss whether Persian involvement is plausible.

events.title: Alexander Consolidates Macedonian Hegemony Over Greece
events.description: In the months following his accession, Alexander moved swiftly to consolidate his control over Macedon and the Greek city-states that had been brought under Macedonian hegemony by his father Philip II. He secured the loyalty of the Macedonian army and nobility, then marched south to Greece to suppress potential revolts. Several Greek cities, including Athens and Thebes, considered rebellion, but Alexander's rapid response and show of force prevented open conflict in 336 BCE. He reaffirmed Macedonian control over the Corinthian League and secured the position of hegemon (leader) that his father had held.
key_figures: Antipater (regent in Macedon during Alexander's absence)

sources.name: Arrian, Anabasis of Alexander, Book I.1

events.certainty_note: The broad outlines of Alexander's consolidation are attested in multiple sources, but specific dates and details of individual city submissions are not always clear. The process likely extended over several months in 336-335 BCE.

events.title: Darius III Assumes Persian Throne
events.description: Following the death of Artaxerxes II and a period of internal dynastic instability, Darius III Codomannus (a distant relative of the royal house) assumed the Persian throne. He was elevated to power by the eunuch Bagoas, who had wielded considerable influence over the previous kings. Darius III's early reign was marked by efforts to consolidate his authority and suppress rebellions in Egypt and other provinces. He was unaware of the full extent of the Macedonian threat under Alexander, as the Persian Empire had long dominated the eastern Mediterranean and Near East. His accession set the stage for the eventual confrontation with Alexander.

sources.name: Justin, Epitome of Pompeius Trogus, X.3

events.certainty_note: The accession of Darius III is attested in Greek sources, but no contemporary Persian sources survive. The date is placed in 336 BCE by historiographic consensus, though some sources suggest 338 BCE. The role of Bagoas and the circumstances of Darius's elevation are described in Greek sources, which may be biased or incomplete.

events.title: Artaxerxes II of Persia Dies; Succession Crisis Begins
events.description: Artaxerxes II, who had reigned for over 40 years, died in 336 BCE. His death triggered a succession crisis within the Persian royal house, as multiple claimants competed for the throne. The eunuch Bagoas, who had wielded considerable power during Artaxerxes II's reign, played a key role in determining the succession. This period of internal instability weakened Persian central authority and distracted the empire from external threats, particularly the rising power of Macedon under Alexander.

sources.name: Justin, Epitome of Pompeius Trogus, X.3

events.certainty_note: The death of Artaxerxes II and the succession crisis are attested in multiple Greek sources. The date (336 BCE) is secure. However, details of the internal Persian political maneuvering come entirely from Greek sources and may be incomplete or biased.

events.title: Qin State Continues Expansion Under King Zheng
events.description: In 336 BCE, the Qin state under King Zheng (later Qin Shi Huang) continued its aggressive expansion and administrative reforms. Qin defeated neighboring states and expanded its territory, consolidating its position as the dominant power in the Warring States period. The Qin's legalist administrative system, military innovations, and economic strength were making it increasingly dominant relative to other Chinese states.
key_figures: King Zheng of Qin (later Qin Shi Huang)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian, Qin Shi Huang benji

events.certainty_note: Chinese chronology for this period is uncertain. The Shiji was compiled centuries after these events. The broader context of Qin expansion is secure, but specific events in 336 BCE are difficult to date precisely.
cross_references: Part of broader Warring States consolidation

disconfirming_evidence: Modern scholarship has challenged several traditional narratives about 336 BCE: (1) The assassination of Philip II is often portrayed as a simple act of personal revenge by Pausanias, but some scholars have suggested Persian involvement or a broader conspiracy. However, evidence for Persian involvement is circumstantial and debated. (2) Alexander's consolidation of power is sometimes presented as inevitable, but modern historians recognize that his position was initially precarious and that his success depended on the loyalty of key generals like Antipater and Parmenion. (3) The Persian succession crisis is often portrayed as evidence of Persian weakness, but modern scholarship recognizes that the Achaemenid Empire remained administratively sophisticated and militarily formidable, despite internal instability. (4) Some sources suggest Darius III's accession occurred in 338 BCE rather than 336 BCE, though 336 BCE is the more widely accepted date.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 336 BCE is heavily dependent on Greek sources, particularly Plutarch, Diodorus Siculus, and Justin, all writing centuries after the events. These sources are generally reliable for major political events but reflect Greek perspectives and may exaggerate or misinterpret Persian affairs. No contemporary Persian sources survive. Chinese sources (Shiji) are much later compilations with chronological uncertainties. The year is well-documented for the Mediterranean and Near East but poorly documented for other regions. The assassination of Philip II is one of the most debated events in ancient history, with scholars continuing to discuss motives and potential conspirators.

graph_edges.note: Philip's assassination forced Alexander to consolidate his power over Macedon and Greece
Alexander's consolidation of Greek hegemony enabled him to secure the northern frontier
Darius III's accession set the stage for his confrontation with Alexander
Artaxerxes II's death triggered the succession crisis that elevated Darius III
Philip's assassination and Alexander's rise initiated the chain of events leading to the Persian invasion
